You are Archon Horizon's Ground agent: the keeper of the project's human-readable mind. You own three things and must leave each true to the project's real state before you finish:
- the blueprints (LaTeX-subset declarations + `\uses` dependency edges),
- the roadmap (the planning index that drives scheduling) — edit it ONLY via the `"$HORIZON_BIN" roadmap` CLI (`set`/`add`/`remove`, `comment <id> --author ground` to log a key advance; pass long prose with `--summary-file` and `--author` on add/set), NEVER by hand-editing the YAML, which corrupts it; the CLI quotes safely,
- memory: durable facts/conventions/dead ends, kept as `memory` inbox items (`horizon inbox add --kind memory --author ground`); prune stale ones with `inbox complete`.
You do NOT do long Lean proof search yourself. You set strategy, keep the blueprints aligned with both the Lean code and the long-term plan, supervise the Horizon agent, and leave it a clear recommendation of what to attempt next (see the recommendation guidance).

# This is a RECONCILE Ground
A Horizon session just ran. Review its work via the project diff (the `project-git` skill) and its report appended below, then reconcile blueprints/roadmap/memory with what actually changed.

The Horizon agent runs free and may leave the workspace messy or its reasoning shaky — you are its supervisor and the workspace's janitor, and you make it correct and tidy again.

How to work:
- On a reconcile round, review the Horizon agent's latest work via its report and the project diff (see the `project-git` skill — projects have no root `.git`; the workspace keeps one out-of-tree ledger, so diff a project by pathspec / by the session's commit trailers). Check that the Lean definitions truly match the blueprint statements, that its reasoning is sound, and that it left no stray files or directories.
- Reconcile blueprints, roadmap status, and memory with what actually changed. Fix blueprint errors, retrieve and add missing references, and keep the DAG dependency-correct.
- When you find a flaw — a wrong proof, a Lean/blueprint mismatch, a dead end — raise an inbox item or comment rather than silently papering over it (see the horizon-inbox skill); use `--author ground` for inbox items/comments you create. ALWAYS write item bodies, comments, roadmap summaries, and reports in Markdown — short paragraphs and `-` bullet lists (never one wall-of-text paragraph), `**bold**` for the key claim, and backticks for ids, lemma names, and files. The inbox is durable coordination, not a progress log: prefer one concise issue/hint over many comments, close obsolete items, retag or merge duplicates, and add a concise closing comment when you `complete` an item.
- When you make a change the human should know about — you restructured projects, took an unexpected direction, or hit something worth flagging — tell them with an `info` inbox item (`horizon inbox add --kind info --author ground`). It is purely a notice and never affects what runs.
- You do not create tasks: tasks are the human's way to launch sessions (and are inferred on demand from a roadmap milestone via `horizon run <roadmap_id>`). To propose work, open an inbox item addressed to the human (`horizon inbox add --to human`); you may `horizon task comment` to suggest an edit, but never add/edit/remove tasks.
- Delegate to subagents to divide the work (blueprint review, diff analysis), preferring a smaller/cheaper model for mechanical checks.
- Keep the roadmap as the project's mathematical status: the main theorems and infrastructure already formalized and still to build, each a milestone that guides the work — it is a map, not a task queue. Update item statuses to match the real Lean/blueprint state, and add milestones for major results that emerge. Before closing/blocking/rejecting a roadmap item, add a concise roadmap comment explaining the evidence and checks. If you comment on a task whose status should be changed by the human, explicitly name the linked `roadmap_refs` that should move with it and the matching status/comment to apply. Marking an item active is guidance, not a command, and does not by itself launch work: a human runs a milestone with `horizon run <roadmap_id>`. If you think work should start, say so in an inbox item to the human.
- Treat the opened inbox below as input for this round only. Preserve frozen/protected state; if an edit would violate it, leave it and flag it.

Blueprint constraints (legible and pure, but mathematically complete — see the blueprint-conventions skill):
- One declaration's worth per node: a short statement + a COMPLETE proof (not a sketch). Keep nodes small by splitting a hard step into its own `\uses`-linked lemma, never by abbreviating the proof to a hand-wave.
- Pure mathematics only: no Lean tactics, no semi-Lean pseudocode, no project history. Do not restate Lean source as prose. Add a `\uses` edge rather than re-explaining a dependency.
- The roadmap is an index, not a journal: one line of summary per item; link, don't recount.
- Memory holds only what is non-obvious and durable: conventions, invariants, dead ends.

# Skills
Invoke the Horizon CLI via the `$HORIZON_BIN` env var (an absolute path), e.g. `"$HORIZON_BIN" inbox list --json` — a bare `horizon` may not be on your shell's PATH.
Most `horizon` commands accept `--json`; use it for AI-friendly output when reading state or ids.
Capability know-how lives in skill files under the workspace `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (Claude Code surfaces them automatically; on other engines read the files directly). Consult the relevant one before acting (exact inbox verbs, lean checks, blueprint conventions, the dependency DAG):
- blueprint-conventions — The LaTeX-subset blueprint format and house style — environments, \uses/\lean/\leanok/\mathlibok/\notready, the math-purity rule, and complete (not sketched) proofs.
- horizon-inbox — Read and act on the Archon Horizon inbox — list/filter agent-ready items, understand labels/kinds, comment, close, create, and message other projects via the `horizon inbox` CLI.
- horizon-waiting — How to wait for slow work (builds, subagents, monitors) inside a one-shot session — block in the foreground and get the result before you report; you are NEVER re-invoked on completion.
- lean-check — Build and check Lean in this workspace and read the errors — use Lean LSP MCP for fast proof-writing feedback, then run faithful kernel verification with `lake build` / `lake env lean`.
- leandag — Consult and refresh the blueprint dependency DAG (leandag) — declaration dependencies, cones, proved status, isolated/dangling nodes, verified union/intersection across projects — via `horizon leandag` and the cached DAG files.
- leansearch — Find existing lemmas/definitions in mathlib, workspace projects, and configured libraries with `horizon search`; use external Mathlib search tools (LeanSearch/Loogle/Moogle/LSP) when broader Mathlib search is needed before writing proofs.
- project-git — How git works in an Archon Horizon workspace — root commits include scoped project files plus shared state, while each project also has an out-of-tree VCS journal for detailed per-round history.
- subagents — How the Ground agent delegates to native subagents to divide review/upkeep work — spawning by name, read-only enforcement, model tiers, and the dynamic descriptor roster.

# Roadmap
- AJC.albanese [active/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Albanese / abelian-variety leg (merges from Albanese) - **Albanese / abelian-variety leg.** The Albanese universal property of $\mathrm{Pic}^{0}$ and the rational-map-extension machinery, developed in `Albanese`, merge back here to supply the abelian-variety structure feeding `AJC.jacobian`.

- AJC.cech [done/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Čech R^i f_* engine (merged) - **Čech higher-direct-image engine (merged, sorry-free).**
$$H^{i}\big(\check{C}^{\bullet}(\mathfrak{U},\mathcal{F})\big)\;\cong\;R^{i}f_{*}\mathcal{F}\qquad(f\ \text{separated quasi-compact, finite affine cover}).$$
`cech_computes_higherDirectImage` + `pushPull` functoriality, proved in `Cech-Cohomology` and merged into AJC (axiom-clean). Residual 16 `sorry` are v4.31 migration-interim, mechanical.

- AJC.fbc [active/proof/high] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Flat base change (Stacks 02KH) - **Flat base change (Stacks 02KH), Čech route.** For flat $g$:
$$g^{*}R^{i}f_{*}\mathcal{F}\;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;R^{i}f'_{*}\,g'^{*}\mathcal{F}.$$
Homology machinery sorry-free (separated, no spectral sequence). Two open leaves: `cechComplex_baseChange_iso` (Stacks 02KG, termwise affine BC via `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso`) and `pullback_preservesFiniteLimits` (flat $g^{*}$ left-exact). See task **T2**.

- AJC.grquot [done/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Grassmannian / Quot representability (merged) - **Relative Grassmannian / Quot representability (merged, sorry-free).**
$$\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)(T)\;\cong\;\{\,V_{T}\twoheadrightarrow Q\ \text{locally free rank }d\,\}\quad(\texttt{Grassmannian.represents}).$$
Plus the SNAP section graded ring/module (Stacks 01CV) and glue-descent atlas. Merged from `GR-Quot-Closure`.

- AJC.jacobian [pending/proof/high] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Jacobian = Albanese (top goal) - **North star.** For a smooth proper geometrically-irreducible curve $C/k$, the Jacobian is an abelian variety representing the degree-$0$ relative Picard functor and realizing the Albanese property:
$$\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}(T)\;=\;\ker\!\big(\mathrm{Pic}(C\times_{k}T)\to\mathrm{Pic}(C)\big),\qquad \dim_{k}\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}=g.$$
Smooth of relative dimension $g$, proper, geometrically irreducible (`exists_unique_ofCurve_comp`).

- AJC.linebundle [done/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Line-bundle comparison-iso / Pic^sharp group (A.1.c.sub) - **Line-bundle comparison-iso substrate (A.1.c.sub).** Gives $\mathrm{Pic}^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ its abelian-group law via the tensor/dual comparison
$$\mathcal{L}\otimes_{\mathcal{O}_C}\mathcal{L}^{-1}\;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;\mathcal{O}_{C}.$$
Finished in `Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso`; merges back into AJC via task **T1** (closes the 5 residual `TensorObjSubstrate`/`PullbackTensorComp` sorries).

- AJC.pic0av [active/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Pic^0 is an abelian variety (A.3) - **$\mathrm{Pic}^{0}$ is an abelian variety (A.3).** Kleiman §5 tangent-space identification, then structure:
$$T_{e}\,\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}\;\cong\;H^{1}(C,\mathcal{O}_{C}),\qquad \dim=g.$$
Remaining: the $k$-linear comparison $\operatorname{Dual}_k(\mathfrak{m}_e/\mathfrak{m}_e^{2})\cong H^{1}(C,\mathcal O_C)$ (`tangentSpaceIso`); then un-invert `smooth` and `proper` (Chevalley–Rosenlicht) and geometric irreducibility.

- AJC.picrep [active/proof/high] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Picard scheme representability cone - **Picard scheme representability (FGA).** The relative Picard functor is representable by a scheme locally of finite type:
$$\underline{\mathrm{Pic}}_{C/k}\ \text{representable};\qquad \mathrm{Quot},\ \mathrm{Grass},\ \text{flattening stratification as inputs}.$$
Cone: `FGAPicRepresentability`, `QuotScheme` endgame, `FlatteningStratification`, `IdentityComponent`.

- AJC.substrate [done/proof/normal] (Algebraic-Jacobian-Challenge) Foundational substrate (Kähler, rigidity, Abel–Jacobi) - **Foundational substrate (sorry-free).** Kähler differentials / cotangent ($\Omega_{C/k}$, cotangent iso, chart algebra), the rigidity lemma, genus theory, Abel–Jacobi scaffolding, and line-bundle coherence/pullback (`RelPicFunctor`, `RelativeSpec`).

- ALB.ab [done/proof/normal] (Albanese) Auslander–Buchsbaum / coheight bridge - **Auslander–Buchsbaum / coheight bridge (sorry-free).** `AuslanderBuchsbaum`, `CoheightBridge`: the commutative-algebra input
$$\operatorname{depth}M+\operatorname{pd}M=\operatorname{depth}R$$
feeding codimension/coheight arguments for the extension theorems.

- ALB.codim1 [active/proof/normal] (Albanese) Codim-one & Thm 3.2 rational-map extension - **Codim-one & Thm 3.2 rational-map extension.** A rational map from a smooth variety to an abelian variety extends over codimension-$1$ points:
$$X\dashrightarrow A\ \Longrightarrow\ X\setminus Z\to A,\ \operatorname{codim}Z\ge 2.$$
`CodimOneExtension` (×3), `Thm32RationalMapExtension` (×1).

- ALB.fga [active/proof/normal] (Albanese) FGA Picard representability slice - **FGA Picard representability slice.** The `FGAPicRepresentability` portion carried in the Albanese extraction that feeds back into the AJC Picard cone.

- ALB.up [active/proof/high] (Albanese) Albanese universal property of Pic^0 - **Albanese universal property of $\mathrm{Pic}^{0}$** (Milne III §6, Prop 6.1). Every pointed map to an abelian variety factors uniquely through the Jacobian:
$$\forall\,\phi:(C,p)\to A,\ \exists!\ \bar{\phi}:\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}\to A\ \text{with}\ \phi=\bar\phi\circ\mathrm{aj}.$$
`AlbaneseUP` (×7): the headline `Pic.albaneseUP` + universal-map descent.

- CECH.acyclic [done/proof/normal] (Cech-Cohomology) Affine acyclicity (Serre vanishing) - **Affine acyclicity (Serre vanishing).**
$$\check{H}^{i}(\mathfrak{U},\mathcal{F})=0\ (i>0)\quad\text{on affine opens},$$
via tilde-vanishing $\Rightarrow$ affine Čech vanishing; localization comparison `AwayComparison`, $\varphi/\varphi_L$ naturality.

- CECH.engine [done/proof/normal] (Cech-Cohomology) Combinatorial / free Čech engine - **Combinatorial / free Čech engine (sorry-free).** Alternating coface complex, homotopy contraction, and exactness of the augmented Čech complex on a cover.

- CECH.main [done/proof/high] (Cech-Cohomology) cech_computes_higherDirectImage (unconditional) - **Comparison theorem `cech_computes_higherDirectImage`.**
$$H^{i}\big(\check{C}^{\bullet}(\mathfrak{U},\mathcal{F})\big)\;\cong\;R^{i}f_{*}\mathcal{F}$$
for separated quasi-compact $f$ and a finite affine cover — unconditional (no enough-injectives appeal). Deliverable merged into AJC.

- CECH.pushpull [done/proof/normal] (Cech-Cohomology) PushPull functoriality - **PushPull functoriality.** `pushPullMap` composition, leg coherence, and the pentagon for the sigma iso over the Čech nerve / wide pullbacks (finitary-extensive distributivity).

- GRQ.cells [done/proof/normal] (GR-Quot-Closure) Grassmannian cells, gluing & descent - **Grassmannian cells, gluing & descent (sorry-free).** Rank-quotient setoid, affine charts, the $\mathrm{GL}_{d}$ transition cocycle, and effective descent gluing the charts into $\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)$.

- GRQ.graded [done/proof/normal] (GR-Quot-Closure) Section graded ring (SNAP) - **Section graded ring (SNAP, sorry-free).** The graded ring/module lanes (Stacks 01CV) and graded Hilbert–Serre rationality:
$$\sum_{n}\dim_{k}M_{n}\,t^{n}\in\mathbb{Z}(t)\ \text{(rational)}.$$

- GRQ.quot [done/proof/normal] (GR-Quot-Closure) Quot scheme (quasi-coherent descent) - **Quot scheme (quasi-coherent descent, sorry-free).** The quasi-coherent-descent machinery of `QuotScheme` retained after carving the χ-blocked Hilbert-polynomial endgame (which stays open in the AJC copy).

- GRQ.represents [done/proof/high] (GR-Quot-Closure) Grassmannian.represents (H^0 leg) - **Representability `Grassmannian.represents` (H^0 leg).**
$$\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\ \text{represents}\ T\mapsto\{\,V_{T}\twoheadrightarrow Q\ \text{rank }d\,\},\qquad \texttt{tautologicalQuotient\_epi}.$$

- LBC.compinv [done/proof/high] (Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso) Terminal comparison inverse (Pic^sharp group law) - **Terminal comparison inverse (deliverable — finished).** The keystone `trivialisation_restrict_compat` and the chart-chase inverse, giving the comparison isomorphism that endows $\mathrm{Pic}^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ with its group structure. Merges back into AJC (**T1**).

- LBC.dual [done/proof/normal] (Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso) Slice-dual transport iso (DUAL route) - **Slice-dual transport iso (DUAL route, sorry-free).**
$$\mathcal{L}^{\vee}|_{U}\;\cong\;(\mathcal{L}|_{U})^{\vee}$$
compatibly across the trivializing slices — `DualInverse`, `DualInverse/SliceTransport`.

- LBC.pullback [done/proof/normal] (Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso) Line-bundle pullback / relative Pic functor - **Line-bundle pullback / relative Pic functor (sorry-free).** `LineBundlePullback`, `RelPicFunctor`; the seed compatibility $f^{*}(\mathcal L\otimes\mathcal M)\cong f^{*}\mathcal L\otimes f^{*}\mathcal M$ on locally trivial bundles (`pullback_tensor_iso_loctriv`).

- LBC.stalktensor [done/proof/normal] (Line-Bundle-Comparison-Iso) Stalk-tensor / internal-hom machinery - **Stalk-tensor / internal-hom machinery (sorry-free).** `TensorObjSubstrate/StalkTensor`, `PresheafInternalHom` — the local model for $\otimes$ and $\mathcal{H}om$ on quasi-coherent sheaves used by the comparison.

- MR.pic [pending/blueprint/normal] (MR0555258-Compactifying-Picard) Compactifying the Picard scheme (D'Souza) - **Compactifying the Picard scheme** (D'Souza, *MR0555258*). Blueprint-stage related-paper formalization: the compactified Jacobian / degenerate Picard variety of a singular curve. Depends on the core AG engine (schemes, cohomology, Picard). Lean target is currently a stub aggregator.

$$e\in\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}=\big(\underline{\mathrm{Pic}}_{C/k}\big)^{\!\circ},\qquad \dim_k\mathrm{Pic}^{0}_{C/k}=g .$$
Builds green (byte-identical to the AJC copies) as an FBC-free, isolated iteration baseline. All 9 residual `sorry` are **substrate-blocked**, not proof-search: the §1 group-scheme leaves (identity-component clopen / subgroup / base change) need Stacks **037Q/04KU** (connected $k$-scheme + $k$-rational section $\Rightarrow$ geometrically connected) and **02LB/04KS** clopen-descent, both absent from v4.31 Mathlib; the $\mathrm{Pic}^{0}$-specific leaves (`Pic0Scheme`, `degree`, `isAbelianVariety`, `finrank_eq_genus`) inherit the FGA sorry-taint (`AJC.picrep`) and the tangent-space comparison (`AJC.pic0av`).

# Subagents
You have native subagents installed for your engine, compiled at run start from the descriptors in `.archon-horizon/subagents/<name>.md` (Claude reads `.claude/agents/`, Codex reads `.codex/agents/`):
- blueprint — Own one assigned blueprint slice end-to-end — keep it pure math with complete proofs, cite references with \source{...} anchors, hold a 1-to-1 correspondence with the Lean, and make its dependency cone finite and clean.
- debug — Diagnose and repair workspace setup/build/environment failures during a run — missing toolchain, broken lakefile, MCP/config/env problems, obscure non-Lean-syntax errors — applying safe fixes or reporting a precise diagnosis.
- janitor — Workspace-hygiene auditor and tidier — keeps the workspace and each subproject navigable, READMEs/roadmap concise and current, files findable, and the inbox from overflowing (capping memory tags, closing what's done).
- page-transcriber — Transcribe a small PDF page range (max 5 pages) into faithful per-page LaTeX using AI VISION on rendered page images (never OCR), each page self-contained with the operators/macros it declares.
- reference-retriever — Fetch original math references into workspace-root references/, register them in references/manifest.yaml, and when needed transcribe PDF page ranges into page-level LaTeX files for blueprint \source{...} anchors.
- work-reviewer [read-only] — Fresh-context review of the previous Horizon agent's work on a task — starts from its git diff, its report, and the task it was given, but is free to dig deeper — then judges whether the work is converging and files concise inbox issues/memory for stuck points and errors.
- blueprint-reviewer [read-only] — Check that the Lean declarations match their blueprint statements and the \uses dependency edges are correct.
- diff-auditor [read-only] — Audit the project diff from the last Horizon round for sound reasoning, no stray files, and no buried sorries.

Delegate by spawning a subagent **by name** through your engine's own subagent mechanism, giving it a focused directive: the slice/scope and the project it applies to. Spawn several in parallel when the work divides cleanly, then wait for and reconcile their reports. Read-only subagents are sandboxed off source edits and report through the `horizon inbox` CLI. You decide whether a subagent fits — use them when they help, skip them when they don't. Each subagent's descriptor pins its own model, usually a cheaper one for mechanical work: prefer delegating routine checks (diff review, reference lookup, lint) to those cheaper subagents and keep your own (more expensive) context for the hard reasoning. To add or change a subagent, edit its descriptor under `.archon-horizon/subagents/` — it recompiles on the next run.

---
name: project-git
description: How git works in an Archon Horizon workspace — root commits include scoped project files plus shared state, while each project also has an out-of-tree VCS journal for detailed per-round history.
---

Read this before running `git diff`/`git log` on a project.

## The model

- **Workspace repo is out-of-tree too** — its git dir is
  `.archon-horizon/vcs/workspace.git` with the workspace **root** as its work tree
  (driven via `--git-dir`/`--work-tree`). **`<root>/.git`** is deliberately not touched, 
  because it is reserved for a user repo. It commits `config.yaml`, scoped project files, and `.archon-horizon/`
  shared state at integration boundaries; never the binary git dirs under
  `.archon-horizon/vcs/`, locks, or other volatile internals.
- **Project repos live out-of-tree.** Every project should have an out-of-tree
  git directory at `.archon-horizon/vcs/<project>.git`, with the project
  directory as its work tree. Project directories do not contain nested `.git/`.
- Expected cadence: both workspace and project repos should be commited at the end of each session, 
  but note that in the case of parallel runs, the commits order might not be sequential, 
  and some files might be modified by other runs, mostly for the workspace repo. However, 
  this should not be a problem in general. 

So a plain `git diff` at the root sees only a user repo (if any), never Archon's.
To inspect Archon's workspace or a project's history, drive its out-of-tree repo
explicitly.

```bash
# Workspace (manifest) history:
git --git-dir=.archon-horizon/vcs/workspace.git --work-tree=. log --oneline -n 20
```

## Reading a project's history / diff

Run from the workspace root:

```bash
GD=.archon-horizon/vcs/<project>.git
WT=<path-to-project>      # the project's `path` from the project list

git --git-dir=$GD --work-tree=$WT log --oneline -n 20
git --git-dir=$GD --work-tree=$WT diff HEAD~1
git --git-dir=$GD --work-tree=$WT diff HEAD~1 -- '*.lean'
git --git-dir=$GD --work-tree=$WT show <sha>
```

To see what the previous Horizon agent changed, diff from the checkpoint before
its session (use `log` to find the boundary).

## Caveats

- Every configured project is expected to have VCS enabled. If
  `.archon-horizon/vcs/<project>.git` is missing, report it as a workspace setup
  issue rather than treating it as normal, but work with it, it might be a new project, or a project that was cloned from the workspace's repo (hence without the vcs committed).
- You read history; you do not commit manually. Horizon checkpoints projects and
  the orchestrator writes the root integration commit.
- The bundled author identity is `Archon Horizon <archon-horizon@local>`.

+\subsection{Descent of connectedness along field extensions}
+\label{subsec:connectedness_descent}
+
+The bridge from connectedness of \(|G^0|\) to geometric connectedness of
+\(G^0 \to \Spec k\) is the classical statement that a connected
+\(k\)-scheme with a \(k\)-rational point is geometrically connected
+(Stacks 04KV, EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14). We prove it from first principles.
+The transcendental heart is the fact that over an algebraically closed
+field the tensor product of two field extensions is a domain
+(Stacks 05P3); the geometric assembly combines universal openness of
+morphisms to the spectrum of a field (Stacks 0383, available in
+mathlib), closedness of integral base changes, and a clopen-image
+argument at the rational point.
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[Evaluation at a maximal ideal over an algebraically closed field]
+  \label{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}
+  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.exists_algHom_ker_eq}
+  \textit{Source: Zariski's lemma; Stacks, tag 00FV.}
+  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(R\) a finite-type
+  \(k\)-algebra, and \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) a maximal ideal. Then
+  there is a \(k\)-algebra homomorphism \(\varphi : R \to k\) with
+  \(\ker \varphi = \mathfrak m\).
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  The quotient \(R/\mathfrak m\) is a field and a finite-type
+  \(k\)-algebra, hence a finite (in particular integral) extension of
+  \(k\) by Zariski's lemma. Since \(k\) is algebraically closed, the
+  structure map \(k \to R/\mathfrak m\) is bijective. Composing the
+  quotient map \(R \to R/\mathfrak m\) with its inverse yields
+  \(\varphi\), whose kernel is \(\mathfrak m\).
+\end{proof}
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[Tensor product with a finite-type domain over an algebraically closed field]
+  \label{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
+  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isDomain_tensorProduct_of_finiteType}
+  \uses{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}
+  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 05P3 (finite-type case).}
+  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(R\) a finite-type
+  \(k\)-algebra which is a domain, and \(A/k\) a field extension. Then
+  \(R \otimes_k A\) is a domain.
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  \uses{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}
+  Nontriviality holds because \(R\) and \(A\) are nontrivial and embed
+  their fraction fields compatibly. Fix a \(k\)-basis \((a_i)\) of
+  \(A\); then \(R \otimes_k A\) is a free \(R\)-module with basis
+  \((1 \otimes a_i)\), so every element \(x\) has unique coordinates
+  \(c_i(x) \in R\). Suppose \(x y = 0\). For every maximal ideal
+  \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\), choose
+  \(\varphi : R \to k\) with kernel \(\mathfrak m\) by
+  \cref{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}, and apply the ring
+  homomorphism \(\varphi \otimes \id : R \otimes_k A \to A\): it sends
+  \(x \mapsto \sum_i \varphi(c_i(x)) a_i\). Since \(A\) is a field,
+  \((\varphi \otimes \id)(x) = 0\) or \((\varphi \otimes \id)(y) = 0\);
+  by linear independence of \((a_i)\) over \(k\) this means all
+  \(c_i(x) \in \mathfrak m\) or all \(c_j(y) \in \mathfrak m\). In
+  either case \(c_i(x)\, c_j(y) \in \mathfrak m\) for all \(i, j\) and
+  all maximal \(\mathfrak m\). A finite-type algebra over a field is a
+  Jacobson ring, and in a Jacobson domain the intersection of all
+  maximal ideals is zero; hence \(c_i(x)\, c_j(y) = 0\) for all
+  \(i, j\). If \(x \neq 0\), some \(c_{i_0}(x) \neq 0\), and since
+  \(R\) is a domain every \(c_j(y) = 0\), i.e.\ \(y = 0\).
+\end{proof}
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[Tensor product of field extensions over an algebraically closed field]
+  \label{lem:tensor_domain_alg_closed}
+  \lean{Algebra.TensorProduct.isDomain_of_isAlgClosed}
+  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
+  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 05P3.}
+  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field and \(A/k\), \(B/k\) field
+  extensions. Then \(A \otimes_k B\) is a domain.
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
+  Suppose \(x y = 0\) with \(x, y \in A \otimes_k B\). Writing \(x\) and
+  \(y\) as finite sums of elementary tensors, let \(R \subseteq A\) be
+  the \(k\)-subalgebra generated by the finitely many first components;
+  \(R\) is a finite-type \(k\)-algebra and a domain (a subring of the
+  field \(A\)). Since \(B\) is free, hence flat, as a \(k\)-module, the
+  natural map \(R \otimes_k B \to A \otimes_k B\) is injective. The
+  elements \(x, y\) lift to \(x', y' \in R \otimes_k B\) with
+  \(x' y' = 0\); by \cref{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
+  (applied with the field extension \(B\)) we get \(x' = 0\) or
+  \(y' = 0\), hence \(x = 0\) or \(y = 0\). Nontriviality is as in
+  \cref{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}.
+\end{proof}
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[The spectrum of a domain is connected]
+  \label{lem:spec_domain_connected}
+  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.connectedSpace_spec_of_isDomain}
+  Let \(R\) be a commutative domain. Then \(\Spec R\) is a connected
+  (indeed irreducible) nonempty topological space.
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  In a domain the nilradical \((0)\) is prime, so \(\Spec R\) is
+  irreducible with generic point \((0)\); irreducible spaces are
+  connected and nonempty.
+\end{proof}
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[Projections from constant-field base changes are geometrically connected]
+  \label{lem:geometrically_connected_projection_alg_closed}
+  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.geometricallyConnected_pullback_fst_of_isAlgClosed}
+  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_alg_closed, lem:spec_domain_connected}
+  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(L/k\) a field extension,
+  and \(f : X \to \Spec k\) a morphism of schemes. Then the projection
+  \(q : X \times_k \Spec L \to X\) is geometrically connected: for every
+  field \(K\) and morphism \(\Spec K \to X\), the fiber product
+  \((X \times_k \Spec L) \times_X \Spec K\) is connected.
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_alg_closed, lem:spec_domain_connected}
+  By the pasting law for fiber products,
+  \((X \times_k \Spec L) \times_X \Spec K \cong \Spec K \times_{\Spec k}
+  \Spec L = \Spec (K \otimes_k L)\), where \(K\) is a field extension of
+  \(k\) via \(\Spec K \to X \to \Spec k\). By
+  \cref{lem:tensor_domain_alg_closed} the ring \(K \otimes_k L\) is a
+  domain, so its spectrum is connected by
+  \cref{lem:spec_domain_connected}.
+\end{proof}
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[Connectedness is stable under base change from an algebraically closed field]
+  \label{lem:connected_base_change_alg_closed}
+  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.connectedSpace_pullback_of_isAlgClosed}
+  \uses{lem:geometrically_connected_projection_alg_closed}
+  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 0363.}
+  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(L/k\) a field extension,
+  and \(f : X \to \Spec k\) a morphism with \(|X|\) connected. Then
+  \(X \times_k \Spec L\) is connected.
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  \uses{lem:geometrically_connected_projection_alg_closed}
+  The projection \(q : X \times_k \Spec L \to X\) is open: it is the
+  base change of \(\Spec L \to \Spec k\), which is universally open
+  because every morphism to the spectrum of a field is universally open
+  (Stacks 0383; available in mathlib). By
+  \cref{lem:geometrically_connected_projection_alg_closed} the
+  fibers of \(q\) over points of \(X\) are connected. An open
+  surjective map with connected fibers onto a connected space has
+  connected total space (mathlib,
+  \(\texttt{GeometricallyConnected.connectedSpace}\)).
+\end{proof}
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[Base change to the algebraic closure preserves connectedness given a section]
+  \label{lem:connected_base_change_algebraic_closure}
+  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.connectedSpace_pullback_algebraicClosure}
+  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 04KV (algebraic step); EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.13.}
+  Let \(k\) be a field, \(f : X \to \Spec k\) a morphism with \(|X|\)
+  connected admitting a section \(s : \Spec k \to X\), and
+  \(\bar k\) an algebraic closure of \(k\). Then
+  \(X \times_k \Spec \bar k\) is connected.
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  The projection \(p : X_{\bar k} := X \times_k \Spec \bar k \to X\) is:
+  open (base change of the universally open \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec
+  k\), as in \cref{lem:connected_base_change_alg_closed}); closed
+  (base change of \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\), which is integral
+  because \(\bar k / k\) is algebraic, and integral morphisms are
+  universally closed); and surjective (base change of a surjection).
+  Let \(x_0 = s(\ast)\) be the image of the section. The section
+  induces a \(k\)-algebra retraction \(\kappa(x_0) \to k\) of the field
+  map \(k \to \kappa(x_0)\); a field homomorphism admitting a
+  retraction is bijective, so \(\kappa(x_0) = k\). Hence the fiber
+  \(p^{-1}(x_0) = \Spec(\kappa(x_0) \otimes_k \bar k) = \Spec \bar k\)
+  is a single point \(\zeta\).

6	import Mathlib

8	/-!
9	# Connected + rational section ⟹ geometrically connected (Stacks 04KV / 037Q)
10	
11	This file proves the descent substrate needed by `Picard/IdentityComponent.lean`:
12	a connected `k`-scheme `X` admitting a `k`-rational section `s : Spec k ⟶ X` of its
13	structural morphism `f : X ⟶ Spec k` is **geometrically connected** (Stacks 04KV,
14	EGA IV₂ 4.5.14). This unblocks the `AJC.picrep` identity-component cone
15	(`identityComponent_geometricallyConnected` and its consumers).
16	
17	## Architecture
18	
19	* **§A Algebra (Stacks 05P3-flavoured).** Over an algebraically closed field `k`,
20	  the tensor product `A ⊗[k] B` of two field extensions is a domain.
21	  - `exists_algHom_ker_eq`: maximal ideals of finite-type `k`-algebras have residue
22	    field `k` (Zariski's lemma + `IsAlgClosed`), packaged as evaluation `k`-algebra
23	    homomorphisms.
24	  - `isDomain_tensorProduct_of_finiteType`: for `R` a finite-type `k`-domain and `A/k`
25	    a field extension, `R ⊗[k] A` is a domain. The classical argument: coordinates of
26	    a zero-divisor pair multiply into every maximal ideal (evaluate via
27	    `exists_algHom_ker_eq` and use linear independence of a `k`-basis of `A`), hence
28	    into the Jacobson radical `= 0` (`R` is a Jacobson domain).
29	  - `Algebra.TensorProduct.isDomain_of_isAlgClosed`: the general case by exhausting
30	    the first factor by finite-type subalgebras (flatness over a field gives
31	    injectivity of `R ⊗[k] B ⟶ A ⊗[k] B`).
32	
33	* **§B Geometry.**
34	  - `connectedSpace_pullback_of_isAlgClosed` (Stacks 0363): for `k` **algebraically
35	    closed** and `X/k` connected, `X ×ₖ Spec L` is connected for every extension
36	    `L/k`. Proof: the projection `X ×ₖ Spec L ⟶ X` is geometrically connected (its
37	    field-valued fibers are `Spec (K ⊗[k] L)`, connected by §A) and open (`Spec L ⟶
38	    Spec k` is universally open, being a morphism to the spectrum of a field), so
39	    Mathlib's `GeometricallyConnected.connectedSpace` applies.
40	  - `connectedSpace_pullback_algebraicClosure` (Stacks 04KV, algebraic step): for `X/k`
41	    connected with a rational section, `X ×ₖ Spec k̄` is connected. The projection
42	    `p : X ×ₖ Spec k̄ ⟶ X` is open, closed (integral) and surjective, and the fiber
43	    over the section point `x₀` is a single point (`κ(x₀) = k`); a nontrivial clopen
44	    partition upstairs would give two clopen sets downstairs both equal to `X`,
45	    contradicting the singleton fiber at `x₀`.
46	  - `geometricallyConnected_of_connectedSpace_of_section` (Stacks 04KV): assembly.
47	    For arbitrary `L/k`, embed `k̄ ↪ L̄ := (AlgebraicClosure L)` over `k`, apply the
48	    two previous steps to get `X ×ₖ Spec L̄` connected, and descend along the
49	    surjection `X ×ₖ Spec L̄ ⟶ X ×ₖ Spec L`.
50	
51	## References
52	
53	- Stacks: 04KV (section ⟹ geometrically connected), 0363 (separably closed base),
54	  05P3 (tensor product of extensions over algebraically closed field is a domain),
55	  0383 (schemes over a field are universally open over it).
56	- EGA IV₂ 4.5.13, 4.5.14.
57	-/

64	open CategoryTheory Limits TensorProduct
65	
66	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
67	
68	/-! ## §A. Algebra: tensor products of field extensions over an algebraically
69	closed field -/
70	
71	section Algebra
72	
73	variable {k : Type*} [Field k] [IsAlgClosed k]
74	
75	/-- **Nullstellensatz evaluation**: a maximal ideal `m` of a finite-type algebra `R`
76	over an algebraically closed field `k` is the kernel of a `k`-algebra homomorphism
77	`R →ₐ[k] k` (the residue field of `m` is `k` by Zariski's lemma). -/
78	theorem exists_algHom_ker_eq (R : Type*) [CommRing R] [Algebra k R]
79	    [Algebra.FiniteType k R] (m : Ideal R) [m.IsMaximal] :
80	    ∃ φ : R →ₐ[k] k, RingHom.ker φ = m := by
81	  -- Install the field structure on the quotient FIRST (instance-ordering trap).
82	  letI : Field (R ⧸ m) := Ideal.Quotient.field m
83	  haveI : Algebra.FiniteType k (R ⧸ m) :=
84	    Algebra.FiniteType.of_surjective (Ideal.Quotient.mkₐ k m)
85	      (Ideal.Quotient.mkₐ_surjective k m)
86	  -- Zariski's lemma: a finite-type field extension is finite, hence integral.
87	  haveI : Module.Finite k (R ⧸ m) := finite_of_finite_type_of_isJacobsonRing k (R ⧸ m)
88	  haveI : Algebra.IsIntegral k (R ⧸ m) := Algebra.IsIntegral.of_finite k (R ⧸ m)
89	  have hbij : Function.Bijective (algebraMap k (R ⧸ m)) :=
90	    IsAlgClosed.algebraMap_bijective_of_isIntegral
91	  let e : k ≃ₐ[k] (R ⧸ m) := AlgEquiv.ofBijective (Algebra.ofId k (R ⧸ m)) hbij
92	  refine ⟨e.symm.toAlgHom.comp (Ideal.Quotient.mkₐ k m), ?_⟩
93	  ext r
94	  rw [RingHom.mem_ker]
95	  change e.symm (Ideal.Quotient.mk m r) = 0 ↔ r ∈ m
96	  rw [map_eq_zero_iff _ e.symm.injective, Ideal.Quotient.eq_zero_iff_mem]
97	
98	/-- **Stacks 05P3, finite-type case**: for `k` algebraically closed, `R` a
99	finite-type `k`-algebra which is a domain, and `A/k` a field extension, the tensor
100	product `R ⊗[k] A` is a domain. -/
101	theorem isDomain_tensorProduct_of_finiteType
102	    (R A : Type*) [CommRing R] [IsDomain R] [Algebra k R] [Algebra.FiniteType k R]
103	    [Field A] [Algebra k A] : IsDomain (R ⊗[k] A) := by
104	  classical
105	  haveI : Nontrivial (R ⊗[k] A) :=
106	    Algebra.TensorProduct.nontrivial_of_algebraMap_injective_of_isDomain k R A
107	      (RingHom.injective _) (RingHom.injective _)
108	  haveI : IsJacobsonRing R := isJacobsonRing_of_finiteType (A := k) (B := R)
109	  haveI : NoZeroDivisors (R ⊗[k] A) := by
110	    refine ⟨@fun x y hxy => ?_⟩
111	    by_contra hcon
112	    push Not at hcon
113	    obtain ⟨hx, hy⟩ := hcon
114	    -- Coordinates with respect to a `k`-basis of `A`.
115	    let bA := Module.Basis.ofVectorSpace k A
116	    let B := Algebra.TensorProduct.basis R bA
117	    -- Any two coordinates (one of `x`, one of `y`) multiply to zero in `R`.
118	    have key : ∀ i j, B.repr x i * B.repr y j = 0 := by
119	      intro i j
120	      -- The product lies in every maximal ideal of `R`.
121	      have hmax : ∀ (m : Ideal R), m.IsMaximal → B.repr x i * B.repr y j ∈ m := by
122	        intro m hm
123	        obtain ⟨φ, hφ⟩ := exists_algHom_ker_eq (k := k) R m
124	        have hmem : ∀ c : R, φ c = 0 → c ∈ m := fun c hc => by
125	          rw [← hφ]; exact RingHom.mem_ker.mpr hc
126	        -- Evaluation `Ψ : R ⊗[k] A →ₐ[k] A` at `m` in the first factor.
127	        let ψR : R →ₐ[k] A := (Algebra.ofId k A).comp φ
128	        let Ψ : R ⊗[k] A →ₐ[k] A :=
129	          Algebra.TensorProduct.lift ψR (AlgHom.id k A) fun r a => Commute.all _ _
130	        -- Ψ in coordinates.
131	        have hΨ : ∀ z : R ⊗[k] A, Ψ z = (B.repr z).sum fun i r => φ r • bA i := by
132	          intro z
133	          conv_lhs => rw [← B.linearCombination_repr z, Finsupp.linearCombination_apply]
134	          rw [map_finsuppSum]
135	          refine Finsupp.sum_congr fun i _ => ?_
136	          rw [Algebra.TensorProduct.basis_repr_symm_apply']
137	          rw [Algebra.TensorProduct.lift_tmul]
138	          simp only [AlgHom.coe_id, id_eq, ψR, AlgHom.coe_comp, Function.comp_apply]
139	          rw [Algebra.ofId_apply, ← Algebra.smul_def]
140	        -- If Ψ z = 0 then every coordinate of `z` is killed by φ.
141	        have hzero : ∀ z : R ⊗[k] A, Ψ z = 0 → ∀ i, φ (B.repr z i) = 0 := by
142	          intro z hz i
143	          by_cases hsupp : i ∈ (B.repr z).support
144	          · have hli := bA.linearIndependent
145	            rw [linearIndependent_iff'] at hli
146	            refine hli (B.repr z).support (fun i => φ (B.repr z i)) ?_ i hsupp
147	            have : ((B.repr z).sum fun i r => φ r • bA i) = 0 := (hΨ z).symm.trans hz
148	            simpa [Finsupp.sum] using this
149	          · rw [Finsupp.notMem_support_iff.mp hsupp, map_zero]
150	        have hmul : Ψ x * Ψ y = 0 := by rw [← map_mul, hxy, map_zero]
151	        rcases mul_eq_zero.mp hmul with h | h
152	        · exact Ideal.mul_mem_right _ _ (hmem _ (hzero x h i))
153	        · exact Ideal.mul_mem_left _ _ (hmem _ (hzero y h j))
154	      -- Jacobson: the intersection of all maximal ideals of `R` is zero.
155	      have hj : B.repr x i * B.repr y j ∈ (⊥ : Ideal R).jacobson :=
156	        Ideal.mem_sInf.mpr fun {J} hJ => hmax J hJ.2
157	      rwa [‹IsJacobsonRing R›.out Ideal.isPrime_bot.isRadical, Ideal.mem_bot] at hj
158	    -- Conclude: `x = 0` or `y = 0`.
159	    obtain ⟨i₀, hi₀⟩ : ∃ i, B.repr x i ≠ 0 := by
160	      by_contra hall
161	      push Not at hall
162	      exact hx (B.repr.map_eq_zero_iff.mp (Finsupp.ext hall))
163	    refine hy (B.repr.map_eq_zero_iff.mp (Finsupp.ext fun j => ?_))
164	    rcases mul_eq_zero.mp (key i₀ j) with h | h
165	    · exact absurd h hi₀
166	    · exact h
167	  exact NoZeroDivisors.to_isDomain _
168	
169	/-- **Stacks 05P3**: for `k` algebraically closed and `A/k`, `B/k` field
170	extensions, the tensor product `A ⊗[k] B` is a domain. In particular
171	`Spec (A ⊗[k] B)` is irreducible, hence connected. -/
172	theorem _root_.Algebra.TensorProduct.isDomain_of_isAlgClosed
173	    (A B : Type*) [Field A] [Algebra k A] [Field B] [Algebra k B] :
174	    IsDomain (A ⊗[k] B) := by
175	  classical
176	  haveI : Nontrivial (A ⊗[k] B) :=
177	    Algebra.TensorProduct.nontrivial_of_algebraMap_injective_of_isDomain k A B
178	      (RingHom.injective _) (RingHom.injective _)
179	  haveI : NoZeroDivisors (A ⊗[k] B) := by
180	    refine ⟨@fun x y hxy => ?_⟩
181	    obtain ⟨sx, hsx⟩ := TensorProduct.exists_finset x
182	    obtain ⟨sy, hsy⟩ := TensorProduct.exists_finset y
183	    -- Adjoin the (finitely many) first components appearing in `x` and `y`.
184	    set S : Finset A := sx.image Prod.fst ∪ sy.image Prod.fst with hS
185	    set R : Subalgebra k A := Algebra.adjoin k (S : Set A) with hRdef
186	    haveI : Algebra.FiniteType k R :=
187	      (Subalgebra.fg_iff_finiteType R).mp (Subalgebra.fg_adjoin_finset S)
188	    -- `R ⊗[k] B ⟶ A ⊗[k] B` is injective (`B` is flat over the field `k`).
189	    let Φ : R ⊗[k] B →ₐ[k] A ⊗[k] B :=
190	      Algebra.TensorProduct.map R.val (AlgHom.id k B)
191	    have hΦinj : Function.Injective Φ := by
192	      have h := Module.Flat.rTensor_preserves_injective_linearMap
193	        (M := B) R.val.toLinearMap Subtype.val_injective
194	      intro a b hab
195	      apply h
196	      simpa [Φ, Algebra.TensorProduct.map, LinearMap.rTensor,
197	        TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.map_eq] using hab
198	    -- Preimages of `x` and `y` in `R ⊗[k] B`.
199	    have hmemx : ∀ p ∈ sx, p.1 ∈ R := fun p hp =>
200	      Algebra.subset_adjoin (Finset.mem_union_left _ (Finset.mem_image_of_mem _ hp))
201	    have hmemy : ∀ p ∈ sy, p.1 ∈ R := fun p hp =>
202	      Algebra.subset_adjoin (Finset.mem_union_right _ (Finset.mem_image_of_mem _ hp))
203	    let x' : R ⊗[k] B := ∑ p ∈ sx.attach, (⟨p.1.1, hmemx p.1 p.2⟩ : R) ⊗ₜ[k] p.1.2
204	    let y' : R ⊗[k] B := ∑ p ∈ sy.attach, (⟨p.1.1, hmemy p.1 p.2⟩ : R) ⊗ₜ[k] p.1.2
205	    have hx' : Φ x' = x := by
206	      rw [hsx]
207	      simp only [x', map_sum]
208	      rw [← Finset.sum_attach sx fun p => p.1 ⊗ₜ[k] p.2]
209	      rfl
210	    have hy' : Φ y' = y := by
211	      rw [hsy]
212	      simp only [y', map_sum]
213	      rw [← Finset.sum_attach sy fun p => p.1 ⊗ₜ[k] p.2]
214	      rfl
215	    -- Push the relation into the finite-type case.
216	    haveI : IsDomain (R ⊗[k] B) := isDomain_tensorProduct_of_finiteType R B
217	    have h0 : x' * y' = 0 := hΦinj (by rw [map_mul, hx', hy', hxy, map_zero])
218	    rcases mul_eq_zero.mp h0 with h | h
219	    · left; rw [← hx', h, map_zero]
220	    · right; rw [← hy', h, map_zero]
221	  exact NoZeroDivisors.to_isDomain _
222	
223	end Algebra
224	
225	/-! ## §B. Geometry: descent of connectedness along field extensions -/
226	
227	section Geometry
228	
229	open CommRingCat
230	
231	/-- The spectrum of a (commutative) domain is connected. -/
232	lemma connectedSpace_spec_of_isDomain (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsDomain R] :
233	    ConnectedSpace ↥(Spec (CommRingCat.of R)) := by
234	  haveI : IrreducibleSpace (PrimeSpectrum R) := PrimeSpectrum.irreducibleSpace
235	  exact inferInstanceAs (ConnectedSpace (PrimeSpectrum R))
236	
237	variable {k : Type u} [Field k] {X : Scheme.{u}}
238	
239	/-- For `k` algebraically closed, the projection `X ×ₖ Spec L ⟶ X` is geometrically
240	connected: its fiber over a `K`-valued point is `Spec (K ⊗[k] L)`, whose ring is a
241	domain by Stacks 05P3. -/
242	theorem geometricallyConnected_pullback_fst_of_isAlgClosed [IsAlgClosed k]
243	    (L : Type u) [Field L] [Algebra k L] (f : X ⟶ Spec (.of k)) :
244	    GeometricallyConnected
245	      (pullback.fst f (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k L)))) := by
246	  set g := Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k L)) with hg
247	  constructor
248	  rw [geometrically_iff_of_isClosedUnderIsomorphisms]
249	  intro K _ y
250	  obtain ⟨φ, hφ⟩ := Spec.map_surjective (y ≫ f)
251	  algebraize [φ.hom]
252	  have hφ' : y ≫ f = Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k K)) := by
253	    rw [← hφ]; congr 1
254	  haveI : IsDomain (K ⊗[k] L) := Algebra.TensorProduct.isDomain_of_isAlgClosed K L
255	  -- `pullback (pullback.fst f g) y ≅ Spec (K ⊗[k] L)`.
256	  let E : pullback (pullback.fst f g) y ≅ Spec (.of (K ⊗[k] L)) :=
257	    pullbackSymmetry _ _ ≪≫ pullbackRightPullbackFstIso f g y ≪≫
258	      pullback.congrHom hφ' rfl ≪≫ pullbackSpecIso k K L
259	  exact E.hom.homeomorph.connectedSpace_iff.mpr
260	    (connectedSpace_spec_of_isDomain (K ⊗[k] L))
261	
262	/-- **Stacks 0363**: over an algebraically closed field `k`, a connected `k`-scheme
263	stays connected after arbitrary base field extension `L/k`. -/
264	theorem connectedSpace_pullback_of_isAlgClosed [IsAlgClosed k]
265	    (L : Type u) [Field L] [Algebra k L] (f : X ⟶ Spec (.of k)) [ConnectedSpace X] :
266	    ConnectedSpace ↥(pullback f (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k L)))) := by
267	  set g := Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k L)) with hg
268	  haveI : IsIntegral (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)) := inferInstance
269	  haveI : Subsingleton ↥(Spec (CommRingCat.of k)) :=
270	    inferInstanceAs (Subsingleton (PrimeSpectrum k))
271	  haveI : UniversallyOpen g := inferInstance
272	  haveI := geometricallyConnected_pullback_fst_of_isAlgClosed L f
273	  exact GeometricallyConnected.connectedSpace (f := pullback.fst f g)
274	    (pullback.fst f g).isOpenMap
275	
276	/-- **Stacks 04KV, algebraic step**: a connected `k`-scheme with a `k`-rational
277	section stays connected after base change to the algebraic closure `k̄/k`. The
278	projection is open, closed and surjective, and the fiber over the section point is
279	a singleton, which forbids nontrivial clopen partitions upstairs. -/
280	theorem connectedSpace_pullback_algebraicClosure
281	    (f : X ⟶ Spec (.of k)) (s : Spec (.of k) ⟶ X) (hsf : s ≫ f = 𝟙 _)
282	    [ConnectedSpace X] :
283	    ConnectedSpace
284	      ↥(pullback f (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k (AlgebraicClosure k))))) := by
285	  set kb := AlgebraicClosure k
286	  set g := Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k kb)) with hg
287	  set p := pullback.fst f g with hp
288	  -- `p` is open.
289	  haveI : IsIntegral (Spec (CommRingCat.of k)) := inferInstance
290	  haveI : Subsingleton ↥(Spec (CommRingCat.of k)) :=
291	    inferInstanceAs (Subsingleton (PrimeSpectrum k))
292	  haveI : UniversallyOpen g := inferInstance
293	  have hopen : IsOpenMap p.base := p.isOpenMap
294	  -- `p` is closed (integral base change).
295	  haveI : IsIntegralHom g := by
296	    rw [IsIntegralHom.SpecMap_iff]
297	    exact Algebra.isIntegral_def.mp inferInstance
298	  have hclosed : IsClosedMap p.base := p.isClosedMap
299	  -- `p` is surjective.
300	  haveI : Nonempty ↥(Spec (CommRingCat.of kb)) :=
301	    inferInstanceAs (Nonempty (PrimeSpectrum kb))
302	  haveI : Surjective g := ⟨Function.surjective_to_subsingleton _⟩
303	  -- The section point and its residue-field retraction.
304	  set x₀ : X := s.base (IsLocalRing.closedPoint k) with hx₀
305	  set ψ : X.residueField x₀ ⟶ .of k :=
306	    X.descResidueField (Scheme.stalkClosedPointTo s) with hψ
307	  have hsplit : Spec.map ψ ≫ X.fromSpecResidueField x₀ = s :=
308	    Scheme.descResidueField_stalkClosedPointTo_fromSpecResidueField k X s
309	  obtain ⟨φ₀, hφ₀⟩ := Spec.map_surjective (X.fromSpecResidueField x₀ ≫ f)
310	  have hretr : φ₀ ≫ ψ = 𝟙 (CommRingCat.of k) := by
311	    rw [← Spec.map_inj, Spec.map_comp, Spec.map_id, hφ₀, ← Category.assoc,
312	      hsplit, hsf]
313	  have hretr' : ∀ a : k, ψ.hom (φ₀.hom a) = a := fun a =>
314	    congrArg (fun (h : CommRingCat.of k ⟶ CommRingCat.of k) => h.hom a) hretr
315	  have hbij : Function.Bijective φ₀.hom := by
316	    refine ⟨RingHom.injective _, fun t => ⟨ψ.hom t, ?_⟩⟩
317	    have : ψ.hom (φ₀.hom (ψ.hom t)) = ψ.hom t := hretr' (ψ.hom t)
318	    exact RingHom.injective ψ.hom this
319	  -- The fiber of `p` over `x₀` is a singleton.
320	  letI : Algebra k ↥(X.residueField x₀) := φ₀.hom.toAlgebra
321	  have hφ₀' : X.fromSpecResidueField x₀ ≫ f =
322	      Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k ↥(X.residueField x₀))) := by
323	    rw [← hφ₀]; congr 1
324	  haveI : Subsingleton ↥(Spec (.of (↥(X.residueField x₀) ⊗[k] kb))) := by
325	    let e1 : k ≃ₐ[k] ↥(X.residueField x₀) :=
326	      AlgEquiv.ofBijective (Algebra.ofId k _) hbij
327	    let e : (↥(X.residueField x₀) ⊗[k] kb) ≃ₐ[k] kb :=
328	      (Algebra.TensorProduct.congr e1.symm AlgEquiv.refl).trans
329	        (Algebra.TensorProduct.lid k kb)
330	    have hf : IsField (↥(X.residueField x₀) ⊗[k] kb) :=
331	      e.toMulEquiv.isField (Field.toIsField kb)
332	    letI := hf.toField
333	    exact inferInstanceAs (Subsingleton (PrimeSpectrum _))
334	  have hfibsub : Subsingleton ↥(p.fiber x₀) := by
335	    let E : p.fiber x₀ ≅ Spec (.of (↥(X.residueField x₀) ⊗[k] kb)) :=
336	      pullbackSymmetry _ _ ≪≫
337	        pullbackRightPullbackFstIso f g (X.fromSpecResidueField x₀) ≪≫
338	        pullback.congrHom hφ₀' rfl ≪≫ pullbackSpecIso k _ kb
339	    exact E.hom.homeomorph.subsingleton
340	  have hfib : ∀ z z' : ↥(pullback f g), p.base z = x₀ → p.base z' = x₀ → z = z' := by
341	    haveI : Subsingleton (p.base ⁻¹' {x₀}) :=
342	      (p.fiberHomeo x₀).symm.toEquiv.subsingleton
343	    intro z z' hz hz'
344	    exact congrArg Subtype.val
345	      (Subsingleton.elim (α := (p.base ⁻¹' {x₀}))
346	        ⟨z, by simp [hz]⟩ ⟨z', by simp [hz']⟩)
347	  -- Nonemptiness: the base-changed section provides a point.
348	  have hne : Nonempty ↥(pullback f g) := by
349	    let sK : Spec (.of kb) ⟶ pullback f g :=
350	      pullback.lift (g ≫ s) (𝟙 _)
351	        (by rw [Category.assoc, hsf, Category.comp_id, Category.id_comp])
352	    exact ⟨sK.base (Nonempty.some inferInstance)⟩
353	  -- Clopen argument.
354	  have hpre : PreconnectedSpace ↥(pullback f g) := by
355	    rw [preconnectedSpace_iff_clopen]
356	    intro T hT
357	    by_cases hTe : T = ∅
358	    · exact Or.inl hTe
359	    right
360	    by_cases hTc : Tᶜ = ∅
361	    · rwa [Set.compl_empty_iff] at hTc
362	    exfalso
363	    have hTne : T.Nonempty := Set.nonempty_iff_ne_empty.mpr hTe
364	    have hTcne : Tᶜ.Nonempty := Set.nonempty_iff_ne_empty.mpr hTc
365	    have hA : IsClopen (p.base '' T) := ⟨hclosed _ hT.isClosed, hopen _ hT.isOpen⟩
366	    have hB : IsClopen (p.base '' Tᶜ) :=
367	      ⟨hclosed _ hT.compl.isClosed, hopen _ hT.compl.isOpen⟩
368	    have hAu : p.base '' T = Set.univ := hA.eq_univ (hTne.image _)
369	    have hBu : p.base '' Tᶜ = Set.univ := hB.eq_univ (hTcne.image _)
370	    obtain ⟨z, hzT, hz⟩ : x₀ ∈ p.base '' T := hAu ▸ Set.mem_univ x₀
371	    obtain ⟨z', hz'T, hz'⟩ : x₀ ∈ p.base '' Tᶜ := hBu ▸ Set.mem_univ x₀
372	    exact hz'T (hfib z z' hz hz' ▸ hzT)
373	  exact { toPreconnectedSpace := hpre, toNonempty := hne }
374	
375	/-- **Stacks 04KV / EGA IV₂ 4.5.14**: a connected `k`-scheme with a `k`-rational
376	section is geometrically connected. -/
377	theorem geometricallyConnected_of_connectedSpace_of_section
378	    (f : X ⟶ Spec (.of k)) (s : Spec (.of k) ⟶ X) (hsf : s ≫ f = 𝟙 _)
379	    [ConnectedSpace X] : GeometricallyConnected f := by
380	  constructor
381	  rw [geometrically_iff_of_commRing_of_isClosedUnderIsomorphisms]
382	  intro L _ _
383	  -- Set up the tower `k ⊆ k̄ ⊆ L̄ ⊇ L`.
384	  set kb := AlgebraicClosure k
385	  set Lb := AlgebraicClosure L
386	  let ι : kb →ₐ[k] Lb := IsAlgClosed.lift
387	  letI : Algebra kb Lb := ι.toRingHom.toAlgebra
388	  -- Step 1: `X ×ₖ Spec k̄` is connected (algebraic step, uses the section).
389	  haveI hZ : ConnectedSpace ↥(pullback f (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k kb)))) :=
390	    connectedSpace_pullback_algebraicClosure f s hsf
391	  -- Step 2: base change from `k̄` to `L̄` (algebraically closed base).
392	  haveI hW : ConnectedSpace
393	      ↥(pullback (pullback.snd f (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k kb))))
394	        (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap kb Lb)))) :=
395	    connectedSpace_pullback_of_isAlgClosed (k := kb) Lb _
396	  -- Step 3: transfer along the pasting isomorphism to `X ×ₖ Spec L̄`.
397	  have hcomp : Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap kb Lb)) ≫
398	      Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k kb)) = Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k Lb)) := by
399	    rw [← Spec.map_comp, ← CommRingCat.ofHom_comp]
400	    congr 2
401	    exact ι.comp_algebraMap
402	  haveI hLb : ConnectedSpace ↥(pullback f (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k Lb)))) :=
403	    ((pullbackLeftPullbackSndIso f (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k kb)))
404	        (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap kb Lb))) ≪≫
405	      pullback.congrHom rfl hcomp).hom.homeomorph.connectedSpace_iff).mp hW
406	  -- Step 4: descend along the surjection `X ×ₖ Spec L̄ ⟶ X ×ₖ Spec L`.
407	  have hLL : Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap L Lb)) ≫
408	      Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k L)) = Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k Lb)) := by
409	    rw [← Spec.map_comp, ← CommRingCat.ofHom_comp, ← IsScalarTower.algebraMap_eq]
410	  haveI hW' : ConnectedSpace
411	      ↥(pullback (pullback.snd f (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k L))))
412	        (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap L Lb)))) :=
413	    ((pullbackLeftPullbackSndIso f (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k L)))
414	        (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap L Lb))) ≪≫
415	      pullback.congrHom rfl hLL).hom.homeomorph.connectedSpace_iff).mpr hLb
416	  haveI : Nonempty ↥(Spec (CommRingCat.of Lb)) :=
417	    inferInstanceAs (Nonempty (PrimeSpectrum Lb))
418	  haveI : Subsingleton ↥(Spec (CommRingCat.of L)) :=
419	    inferInstanceAs (Subsingleton (PrimeSpectrum L))
420	  haveI : Surjective (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap L Lb))) :=
421	    ⟨Function.surjective_to_subsingleton _⟩
422	  set π := pullback.fst (pullback.snd f (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k L))))
423	    (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap L Lb))) with hπ
424	  haveI : Surjective π := inferInstance
425	  have hsurj : Function.Surjective π.base := π.surjective
426	  have hpre : _root_.IsPreconnected
427	      (Set.univ : Set ↥(pullback f (Spec.map (ofHom (algebraMap k L))))) := by
428	    have h1 := connectedSpace_iff_univ.mp hW'
429	    have h2 := h1.isPreconnected.image π.base
430	      (Continuous.continuousOn (by fun_prop))
431	    rwa [Set.image_univ, Set.range_eq_univ.mpr hsurj] at h2
432	  exact { toPreconnectedSpace := ⟨hpre⟩,
433	          toNonempty := ⟨π.base (Nonempty.some hW'.toNonempty)⟩ }
434	
435	end Geometry
436	
437	end AlgebraicGeometry

295	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
296	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
297	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
298	\end{proof}
299	
300	\subsection{Descent of connectedness along field extensions}
301	\label{subsec:connectedness_descent}
302	
303	The bridge from connectedness of \(|G^0|\) to geometric connectedness of
304	\(G^0 \to \Spec k\) is the classical statement that a connected
305	\(k\)-scheme with a \(k\)-rational point is geometrically connected
306	(Stacks 04KV, EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14). We prove it from first principles.
307	The transcendental heart is the fact that over an algebraically closed
308	field the tensor product of two field extensions is a domain
309	(Stacks 05P3); the geometric assembly combines universal openness of
310	morphisms to the spectrum of a field (Stacks 0383, available in
311	mathlib), closedness of integral base changes, and a clopen-image
312	argument at the rational point.
313	
314	\begin{lemma}
315	
316	\leanok
317	[Evaluation at a maximal ideal over an algebraically closed field]
318	  \label{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}
319	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.exists_algHom_ker_eq}
320	  \textit{Source: Zariski's lemma; Stacks, tag 00FV.}
321	  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(R\) a finite-type
322	  \(k\)-algebra, and \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) a maximal ideal. Then
323	  there is a \(k\)-algebra homomorphism \(\varphi : R \to k\) with
324	  \(\ker \varphi = \mathfrak m\).
325	\end{lemma}
326	\begin{proof}
327	  \leanok
328	  The quotient \(R/\mathfrak m\) is a field and a finite-type
329	  \(k\)-algebra, hence a finite (in particular integral) extension of
330	  \(k\) by Zariski's lemma. Since \(k\) is algebraically closed, the
331	  structure map \(k \to R/\mathfrak m\) is bijective. Composing the
332	  quotient map \(R \to R/\mathfrak m\) with its inverse yields
333	  \(\varphi\), whose kernel is \(\mathfrak m\).
334	\end{proof}
335	
336	\begin{lemma}
337	
338	\leanok
339	[Tensor product with a finite-type domain over an algebraically closed field]
340	  \label{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
341	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isDomain_tensorProduct_of_finiteType}
342	  \uses{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}
343	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 05P3 (finite-type case).}
344	  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(R\) a finite-type
345	  \(k\)-algebra which is a domain, and \(A/k\) a field extension. Then
346	  \(R \otimes_k A\) is a domain.
347	\end{lemma}
348	\begin{proof}
349	  \leanok
350	  \uses{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}
351	  Nontriviality holds because \(R\) and \(A\) are nontrivial and embed
352	  their fraction fields compatibly. Fix a \(k\)-basis \((a_i)\) of
353	  \(A\); then \(R \otimes_k A\) is a free \(R\)-module with basis
354	  \((1 \otimes a_i)\), so every element \(x\) has unique coordinates
355	  \(c_i(x) \in R\). Suppose \(x y = 0\). For every maximal ideal
356	  \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\), choose
357	  \(\varphi : R \to k\) with kernel \(\mathfrak m\) by
358	  \cref{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}, and apply the ring
359	  homomorphism \(\varphi \otimes \id : R \otimes_k A \to A\): it sends
360	  \(x \mapsto \sum_i \varphi(c_i(x)) a_i\). Since \(A\) is a field,
361	  \((\varphi \otimes \id)(x) = 0\) or \((\varphi \otimes \id)(y) = 0\);
362	  by linear independence of \((a_i)\) over \(k\) this means all
363	  \(c_i(x) \in \mathfrak m\) or all \(c_j(y) \in \mathfrak m\). In
364	  either case \(c_i(x)\, c_j(y) \in \mathfrak m\) for all \(i, j\) and
365	  all maximal \(\mathfrak m\). A finite-type algebra over a field is a
366	  Jacobson ring, and in a Jacobson domain the intersection of all
367	  maximal ideals is zero; hence \(c_i(x)\, c_j(y) = 0\) for all
368	  \(i, j\). If \(x \neq 0\), some \(c_{i_0}(x) \neq 0\), and since
369	  \(R\) is a domain every \(c_j(y) = 0\), i.e.\ \(y = 0\).
370	\end{proof}
371	
372	\begin{lemma}
373	
374	\leanok
375	[Tensor product of field extensions over an algebraically closed field]
376	  \label{lem:tensor_domain_alg_closed}
377	  \lean{Algebra.TensorProduct.isDomain_of_isAlgClosed}
378	  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
379	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 05P3.}
380	  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field and \(A/k\), \(B/k\) field
381	  extensions. Then \(A \otimes_k B\) is a domain.
382	\end{lemma}
383	\begin{proof}
384	  \leanok
385	  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
386	  Suppose \(x y = 0\) with \(x, y \in A \otimes_k B\). Writing \(x\) and
387	  \(y\) as finite sums of elementary tensors, let \(R \subseteq A\) be
388	  the \(k\)-subalgebra generated by the finitely many first components;
389	  \(R\) is a finite-type \(k\)-algebra and a domain (a subring of the
390	  field \(A\)). Since \(B\) is free, hence flat, as a \(k\)-module, the
391	  natural map \(R \otimes_k B \to A \otimes_k B\) is injective. The
392	  elements \(x, y\) lift to \(x', y' \in R \otimes_k B\) with
393	  \(x' y' = 0\); by \cref{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
394	  (applied with the field extension \(B\)) we get \(x' = 0\) or
395	  \(y' = 0\), hence \(x = 0\) or \(y = 0\). Nontriviality is as in
396	  \cref{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}.
397	\end{proof}
398	
399	\begin{lemma}
400	
401	\leanok
402	[The spectrum of a domain is connected]
403	  \label{lem:spec_domain_connected}
404	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.connectedSpace_spec_of_isDomain}
405	  Let \(R\) be a commutative domain. Then \(\Spec R\) is a connected
406	  (indeed irreducible) nonempty topological space.
407	\end{lemma}
408	\begin{proof}
409	  \leanok
410	  In a domain the nilradical \((0)\) is prime, so \(\Spec R\) is
411	  irreducible with generic point \((0)\); irreducible spaces are
412	  connected and nonempty.
413	\end{proof}
414	
415	\begin{lemma}
416	
417	\leanok
418	[Projections from constant-field base changes are geometrically connected]
419	  \label{lem:geometrically_connected_projection_alg_closed}
420	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.geometricallyConnected_pullback_fst_of_isAlgClosed}
421	  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_alg_closed, lem:spec_domain_connected}
422	  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(L/k\) a field extension,
423	  and \(f : X \to \Spec k\) a morphism of schemes. Then the projection
424	  \(q : X \times_k \Spec L \to X\) is geometrically connected: for every
425	  field \(K\) and morphism \(\Spec K \to X\), the fiber product
426	  \((X \times_k \Spec L) \times_X \Spec K\) is connected.
427	\end{lemma}
428	\begin{proof}
429	  \leanok
430	  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_alg_closed, lem:spec_domain_connected}
431	  By the pasting law for fiber products,
432	  \((X \times_k \Spec L) \times_X \Spec K \cong \Spec K \times_{\Spec k}
433	  \Spec L = \Spec (K \otimes_k L)\), where \(K\) is a field extension of
434	  \(k\) via \(\Spec K \to X \to \Spec k\). By
435	  \cref{lem:tensor_domain_alg_closed} the ring \(K \otimes_k L\) is a
436	  domain, so its spectrum is connected by
437	  \cref{lem:spec_domain_connected}.
438	\end{proof}
439	
440	\begin{lemma}
441	
442	\leanok
443	[Connectedness is stable under base change from an algebraically closed field]
444	  \label{lem:connected_base_change_alg_closed}
445	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.connectedSpace_pullback_of_isAlgClosed}
446	  \uses{lem:geometrically_connected_projection_alg_closed}
447	  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 0363.}
448	  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(L/k\) a field extension,
449	  and \(f : X \to \Spec k\) a morphism with \(|X|\) connected. Then
450	  \(X \times_k \Spec L\) is connected.
451	\end{lemma}
452	\begin{proof}
453	  \leanok
454	  \uses{lem:geometrically_connected_projection_alg_closed}
455	  The projection \(q : X \times_k \Spec L \to X\) is open: it is the
456	  base change of \(\Spec L \to \Spec k\), which is universally open
457	  because every morphism to the spectrum of a field is universally open
458	  (Stacks 0383; available in mathlib). By
459	  \cref{lem:geometrically_connected_projection_alg_closed} the
460	  fibers of \(q\) over points of \(X\) are connected. An open
461	  surjective map with connected fibers onto a connected space has
462	  connected total space (mathlib,
463	  \(\texttt{GeometricallyConnected.connectedSpace}\)).

469	[Base change to the algebraic closure preserves connectedness given a section]
470	  \label{lem:connected_base_change_algebraic_closure}

473	  Let \(k\) be a field, \(f : X \to \Spec k\) a morphism with \(|X|\)
474	  connected admitting a section \(s : \Spec k \to X\), and
475	  \(\bar k\) an algebraic closure of \(k\). Then
476	  \(X \times_k \Spec \bar k\) is connected.
477	\end{lemma}
478	\begin{proof}
479	  \leanok
480	  The projection \(p : X_{\bar k} := X \times_k \Spec \bar k \to X\) is:
481	  open (base change of the universally open \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec
482	  k\), as in \cref{lem:connected_base_change_alg_closed}); closed
483	  (base change of \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\), which is integral
484	  because \(\bar k / k\) is algebraic, and integral morphisms are
485	  universally closed); and surjective (base change of a surjection).
486	  Let \(x_0 = s(\ast)\) be the image of the section. The section
487	  induces a \(k\)-algebra retraction \(\kappa(x_0) \to k\) of the field
488	  map \(k \to \kappa(x_0)\); a field homomorphism admitting a
489	  retraction is bijective, so \(\kappa(x_0) = k\). Hence the fiber
490	  \(p^{-1}(x_0) = \Spec(\kappa(x_0) \otimes_k \bar k) = \Spec \bar k\)
491	  is a single point \(\zeta\).
492	
493	  \(X_{\bar k}\) is nonempty (it contains \(\zeta\), or equivalently
494	  the image of the base-changed section). Let \(T \subseteq
495	  X_{\bar k}\) be clopen with \(T \neq \emptyset\) and
496	  \(T^{\mathsf c} \neq \emptyset\). Since \(p\) is both open and
497	  closed, \(p(T)\) and \(p(T^{\mathsf c})\) are nonempty clopen subsets
498	  of the connected space \(|X|\), hence both equal \(|X|\). In
499	  particular \(x_0 \in p(T) \cap p(T^{\mathsf c})\), so the fiber
500	  \(p^{-1}(x_0)\) meets both \(T\) and \(T^{\mathsf c}\); but that
501	  fiber is the single point \(\zeta\) — a contradiction. So

533	  surjective (base change of the surjection

---
name: leandag
description: Consult and refresh the blueprint dependency DAG (leandag) — declaration dependencies, cones, proved status, isolated/dangling nodes, verified union/intersection across projects — via `horizon leandag` and the cached DAG files.
---

The DAG links blueprint nodes by `\uses` edges. An edge `source -> target` means
*target depends on source*. The engine records each node's `\lean` link,
`proved`/`leanok` status, effort metrics, and Lean source metadata.

## Keep It Fresh

- Run the project build after Lean/blueprint edits so generated Lean artifacts and
  imported libraries are current.
- Run `horizon blueprint` after blueprint or Lean declaration changes; it writes
  `.archon-horizon/blueprints/<project>.json` for the dashboard and cached DAG
  consumers.
- Use `horizon leandag` for live inspection when deciding what to prove next.

## `horizon leandag` (preferred)

- `horizon leandag` — per-project summary: nodes, edges, proved count, and any
  isolated nodes (no edge in or out — usually a blueprint bug to fix).
- `horizon leandag -p <project> --node <label>` — one node's direct deps, full
  transitive cone, and reverse deps.
- `horizon leandag -p a -p b --union` — merge projects by declaration/node id.
  Same-id nodes are combined; divergent non-signature metadata is tolerated, but
  a Lean signature mismatch is an error to investigate.
- `horizon leandag -p a -p b --intersect` — only shared declaration/node ids,
  with the same signature check.
- Add `--json` for machine-readable output.

A node is "ready" when all dependencies are proved. Prefer ready nodes when
choosing what to attempt next.

## Cached JSON

`horizon blueprint` writes each project's DAG to
`.archon-horizon/blueprints/<project>.json` (`nodes`, `edges`, `dangling`) for
the dashboard. Under the leandag engine, `\uses` are resolved at build/cache
time, so `dangling` is empty there; the parser fallback still populates it. To
check for orphan/unresolved nodes, use `horizon leandag` rather than relying only
on `dangling`.

+\subsection{Descent of connectedness along field extensions}
+\label{subsec:connectedness_descent}
+
+The bridge from connectedness of \(|G^0|\) to geometric connectedness of
+\(G^0 \to \Spec k\) is the classical statement that a connected
+\(k\)-scheme with a \(k\)-rational point is geometrically connected
+(Stacks 04KV, EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14). We prove it from first principles.
+The transcendental heart is the fact that over an algebraically closed
+field the tensor product of two field extensions is a domain
+(Stacks 05P3); the geometric assembly combines universal openness of
+morphisms to the spectrum of a field (Stacks 0383, available in
+mathlib), closedness of integral base changes, and a clopen-image
+argument at the rational point.
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[Evaluation at a maximal ideal over an algebraically closed field]
+  \label{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}
+  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.exists_algHom_ker_eq}
+  \textit{Source: Zariski's lemma; Stacks, tag 00FV.}
+  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(R\) a finite-type
+  \(k\)-algebra, and \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) a maximal ideal. Then
+  there is a \(k\)-algebra homomorphism \(\varphi : R \to k\) with
+  \(\ker \varphi = \mathfrak m\).
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  The quotient \(R/\mathfrak m\) is a field and a finite-type
+  \(k\)-algebra, hence a finite (in particular integral) extension of
+  \(k\) by Zariski's lemma. Since \(k\) is algebraically closed, the
+  structure map \(k \to R/\mathfrak m\) is bijective. Composing the
+  quotient map \(R \to R/\mathfrak m\) with its inverse yields
+  \(\varphi\), whose kernel is \(\mathfrak m\).
+\end{proof}
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[Tensor product with a finite-type domain over an algebraically closed field]
+  \label{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
+  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.isDomain_tensorProduct_of_finiteType}
+  \uses{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}
+  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 05P3 (finite-type case).}
+  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(R\) a finite-type
+  \(k\)-algebra which is a domain, and \(A/k\) a field extension. Then
+  \(R \otimes_k A\) is a domain.
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  \uses{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}
+  Nontriviality holds because \(R\) and \(A\) are nontrivial and embed
+  their fraction fields compatibly. Fix a \(k\)-basis \((a_i)\) of
+  \(A\); then \(R \otimes_k A\) is a free \(R\)-module with basis
+  \((1 \otimes a_i)\), so every element \(x\) has unique coordinates
+  \(c_i(x) \in R\). Suppose \(x y = 0\). For every maximal ideal
+  \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\), choose
+  \(\varphi : R \to k\) with kernel \(\mathfrak m\) by
+  \cref{lem:maximal_ideal_evaluation_alg_closed}, and apply the ring
+  homomorphism \(\varphi \otimes \id : R \otimes_k A \to A\): it sends
+  \(x \mapsto \sum_i \varphi(c_i(x)) a_i\). Since \(A\) is a field,
+  \((\varphi \otimes \id)(x) = 0\) or \((\varphi \otimes \id)(y) = 0\);
+  by linear independence of \((a_i)\) over \(k\) this means all
+  \(c_i(x) \in \mathfrak m\) or all \(c_j(y) \in \mathfrak m\). In
+  either case \(c_i(x)\, c_j(y) \in \mathfrak m\) for all \(i, j\) and
+  all maximal \(\mathfrak m\). A finite-type algebra over a field is a
+  Jacobson ring, and in a Jacobson domain the intersection of all
+  maximal ideals is zero; hence \(c_i(x)\, c_j(y) = 0\) for all
+  \(i, j\). If \(x \neq 0\), some \(c_{i_0}(x) \neq 0\), and since
+  \(R\) is a domain every \(c_j(y) = 0\), i.e.\ \(y = 0\).
+\end{proof}
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[Tensor product of field extensions over an algebraically closed field]
+  \label{lem:tensor_domain_alg_closed}
+  \lean{Algebra.TensorProduct.isDomain_of_isAlgClosed}
+  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
+  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 05P3.}
+  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field and \(A/k\), \(B/k\) field
+  extensions. Then \(A \otimes_k B\) is a domain.
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
+  Suppose \(x y = 0\) with \(x, y \in A \otimes_k B\). Writing \(x\) and
+  \(y\) as finite sums of elementary tensors, let \(R \subseteq A\) be
+  the \(k\)-subalgebra generated by the finitely many first components;
+  \(R\) is a finite-type \(k\)-algebra and a domain (a subring of the
+  field \(A\)). Since \(B\) is free, hence flat, as a \(k\)-module, the
+  natural map \(R \otimes_k B \to A \otimes_k B\) is injective. The
+  elements \(x, y\) lift to \(x', y' \in R \otimes_k B\) with
+  \(x' y' = 0\); by \cref{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}
+  (applied with the field extension \(B\)) we get \(x' = 0\) or
+  \(y' = 0\), hence \(x = 0\) or \(y = 0\). Nontriviality is as in
+  \cref{lem:tensor_domain_finite_type_alg_closed}.
+\end{proof}
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[The spectrum of a domain is connected]
+  \label{lem:spec_domain_connected}
+  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.connectedSpace_spec_of_isDomain}
+  Let \(R\) be a commutative domain. Then \(\Spec R\) is a connected
+  (indeed irreducible) nonempty topological space.
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  In a domain the nilradical \((0)\) is prime, so \(\Spec R\) is
+  irreducible with generic point \((0)\); irreducible spaces are
+  connected and nonempty.
+\end{proof}
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[Projections from constant-field base changes are geometrically connected]
+  \label{lem:geometrically_connected_projection_alg_closed}
+  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.geometricallyConnected_pullback_fst_of_isAlgClosed}
+  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_alg_closed, lem:spec_domain_connected}
+  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(L/k\) a field extension,
+  and \(f : X \to \Spec k\) a morphism of schemes. Then the projection
+  \(q : X \times_k \Spec L \to X\) is geometrically connected: for every
+  field \(K\) and morphism \(\Spec K \to X\), the fiber product
+  \((X \times_k \Spec L) \times_X \Spec K\) is connected.
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  \uses{lem:tensor_domain_alg_closed, lem:spec_domain_connected}
+  By the pasting law for fiber products,
+  \((X \times_k \Spec L) \times_X \Spec K \cong \Spec K \times_{\Spec k}
+  \Spec L = \Spec (K \otimes_k L)\), where \(K\) is a field extension of
+  \(k\) via \(\Spec K \to X \to \Spec k\). By
+  \cref{lem:tensor_domain_alg_closed} the ring \(K \otimes_k L\) is a
+  domain, so its spectrum is connected by
+  \cref{lem:spec_domain_connected}.
+\end{proof}
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[Connectedness is stable under base change from an algebraically closed field]
+  \label{lem:connected_base_change_alg_closed}
+  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.connectedSpace_pullback_of_isAlgClosed}
+  \uses{lem:geometrically_connected_projection_alg_closed}
+  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 0363.}
+  Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, \(L/k\) a field extension,
+  and \(f : X \to \Spec k\) a morphism with \(|X|\) connected. Then
+  \(X \times_k \Spec L\) is connected.
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  \uses{lem:geometrically_connected_projection_alg_closed}
+  The projection \(q : X \times_k \Spec L \to X\) is open: it is the
+  base change of \(\Spec L \to \Spec k\), which is universally open
+  because every morphism to the spectrum of a field is universally open
+  (Stacks 0383; available in mathlib). By
+  \cref{lem:geometrically_connected_projection_alg_closed} the
+  fibers of \(q\) over points of \(X\) are connected. An open
+  surjective map with connected fibers onto a connected space has
+  connected total space (mathlib,
+  \(\texttt{GeometricallyConnected.connectedSpace}\)).
+\end{proof}
+
+\begin{lemma}
+
+\leanok
+[Base change to the algebraic closure preserves connectedness given a section]
+  \label{lem:connected_base_change_algebraic_closure}
+  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.connectedSpace_pullback_algebraicClosure}
+  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 04KV (algebraic step); EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.13.}
+  Let \(k\) be a field, \(f : X \to \Spec k\) a morphism with \(|X|\)
+  connected admitting a section \(s : \Spec k \to X\), and
+  \(\bar k\) an algebraic closure of \(k\). Then
+  \(X \times_k \Spec \bar k\) is connected.
+\end{lemma}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  The projection \(p : X_{\bar k} := X \times_k \Spec \bar k \to X\) is:
+  open (base change of the universally open \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec
+  k\), as in \cref{lem:connected_base_change_alg_closed}); closed
+  (base change of \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\), which is integral
+  because \(\bar k / k\) is algebraic, and integral morphisms are
+  universally closed); and surjective (base change of a surjection).
+  Let \(x_0 = s(\ast)\) be the image of the section. The section
+  induces a \(k\)-algebra retraction \(\kappa(x_0) \to k\) of the field
+  map \(k \to \kappa(x_0)\); a field homomorphism admitting a
+  retraction is bijective, so \(\kappa(x_0) = k\). Hence the fiber
+  \(p^{-1}(x_0) = \Spec(\kappa(x_0) \otimes_k \bar k) = \Spec \bar k\)
+  is a single point \(\zeta\).
+
+  \(X_{\bar k}\) is nonempty (it contains \(\zeta\), or equivalently
+  the image of the base-changed section). Let \(T \subseteq
+  X_{\bar k}\) be clopen with \(T \neq \emptyset\) and
+  \(T^{\mathsf c} \neq \emptyset\). Since \(p\) is both open and
+  closed, \(p(T)\) and \(p(T^{\mathsf c})\) are nonempty clopen subsets
+  of the connected space \(|X|\), hence both equal \(|X|\). In
+  particular \(x_0 \in p(T) \cap p(T^{\mathsf c})\), so the fiber
+  \(p^{-1}(x_0)\) meets both \(T\) and \(T^{\mathsf c}\); but that
+  fiber is the single point \(\zeta\) — a contradiction. So
+  \(X_{\bar k}\) admits no nontrivial clopen partition and is
+  connected.
+\end{proof}
+
+\begin{theorem}
+
+\leanok
+[Connected with a rational section implies geometrically connected]
+  \label{thm:geometrically_connected_of_section}
+  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.geometricallyConnected_of_connectedSpace_of_section}
+  \uses{lem:connected_base_change_algebraic_closure, lem:connected_base_change_alg_closed}
+  \textit{Source: Stacks, tag 04KV; EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14.}
+  Let \(k\) be a field and \(f : X \to \Spec k\) a morphism with
+  \(|X|\) connected, admitting a section \(s : \Spec k \to X\). Then
+  \(f\) is geometrically connected: for every field extension \(L/k\)
+  the base change \(X \times_k \Spec L\) is connected.
+\end{theorem}
+\begin{proof}
+  \leanok
+  \uses{lem:connected_base_change_algebraic_closure, lem:connected_base_change_alg_closed}
+  Let \(L/k\) be arbitrary, \(\bar L\) an algebraic closure of \(L\),
+  and \(\bar k\) an algebraic closure of \(k\). Since \(\bar k/k\) is
+  algebraic and \(\bar L\) is algebraically closed, there is a
+  \(k\)-embedding \(\bar k \hookrightarrow \bar L\). By
+  \cref{lem:connected_base_change_algebraic_closure},
+  \(X_{\bar k}\) is connected; by
+  \cref{lem:connected_base_change_alg_closed} applied over the
+  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) to the extension
+  \(\bar L / \bar k\), the scheme
+  \(X_{\bar L} = X_{\bar k} \times_{\bar k} \Spec \bar L\) is
+  connected. Finally the projection \(X_{\bar L} \to X_L\) is
+  surjective (base change of the surjection
+  \(\Spec \bar L \to \Spec L\)) and continuous, so \(X_L\) is the
+  continuous image of a connected space, hence connected.
+\end{proof}
+
